title: ISSS608 Visual Analytics and Applications
text:
SMU MITB · ISSS608 · AY2025/26
  Visual Analytics & Applications
  
    A portfolio of data visualisation work exploring interactive graphics, statistical analysis,
    and visual storytelling with R and Tableau.
  
  
    
      20
      Hands-on Exercises
    
    
      2
      Major Projects
    
    
      13
      Tableau Dashboards
    
    
      1
      Shiny App
    
  





  
    
    Take-home Exercises
    2 projects
    ›
  

  
    

      
        Project 01
        Motor Insurance Portfolio Analysis
        
          Univariate and bivariate visual analytics of a Spanish motor insurance portfolio
          (2022–2024), examining driver profiles, vehicle characteristics, premiums, claims,
          and loss ratios using ggplot2 and tidyverse.
        
        
          Executive Summary
          Technical Report
        
      

      
        Project 02
        VAST Challenge 2026 MC1 — TenantThread Crisis
        
          A multi-agent visual analytics investigation into the TenantThread embargo breach
          using network analysis, text visualisation, and interactive graphics to uncover
          communication patterns and key actors.
        
        
          Analysis Report
          Shiny App
        
      

    
  





  
    
    Group Project
    1 project
    ›
  

  
    

      
        Group Project
        VAST Challenge 2026 MC1 — TenantThread Investigation
        
          A collaborative visual analytics investigation of the VAST Challenge 2026
          Mini-Challenge 1, delivered as an interactive web application.
        
        
          Group Project ↗
        
      

    
  





  
    
    Hands-on Exercises
    20 exercises
    ›
  

  

    
    
      Chapter 1–2 · Introduction
      
        
          Exercise 1
          Introduction to Visual Analytics with ggplot2
          →
        
        
          Exercise 2
          Designing Graphs to Enlighten
          →
        
      
    

    
    
      Chapter 3 · Interactivity in Visual Analytics
      
        
          Exercise 3a
          Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
          →
        
        
          Exercise 3b
          Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
          →
        
      
    

    
    
      Chapter 4 · Fundamentals of Visual Analytics
      
        
          Exercise 4a
          Visualising Distribution
          →
        
        
          Exercise 4b
          Visual Statistical Analysis
          →
        
        
          Exercise 4c
          Visualising Uncertainty
          →
        
        
          Exercise 4d
          Building Funnel Plots with R
          →
        
      
    

    
    
      Chapter 5 · Visual Multivariate Analysis
      
        
          Exercise 5a
          Building Ternary Plots with R
          →
        
        
          Exercise 5b
          Visual Correlation Analysis
          →

      Chapter 9 · Visualising and Analysing Geographic Data

          Exercise 9b
          Visualising Geospatial Point Data
          →

          Analytical Mapping
          →
        
      
    

    
    
      Chapter 10 · Information Dashboard Design

          Information Dashboard Design

title: Tableau Resources
text:
A curated collection of helpful links related to Tableau — official documentation,
  white papers, inspiring blogs, webinars, and tips used throughout ISSS608 Visual
  Analytics and Applications.




  
    
    All About Tableau
  
  

    
      📊
      
        Tableau Home Page
        The official Tableau website — product information, downloads, and the latest news.
      
    

    
      🎓
      
        Training and Tutorials
        Free on-demand and live training videos covering Tableau from beginner to advanced.
      
    

    
      📖
      
        Tableau Desktop User Guide
        The complete reference manual for building visualisations and dashboards in Tableau Desktop.
      
    

    
      🧹
      
        Tableau Prep Builder User Guide
        Documentation for cleaning, shaping, and combining data with Tableau Prep Builder.
      
    

    
      🖼️
      
        Visual Gallery
        A showcase of standout visualisations and dashboards for inspiration.
      
    

    
      🌐
      
        Tableau Public
        The free platform for publishing and exploring interactive visualisations from the community.
      
    

  




  
    
    White Papers
  
  

    
      📄
      
        10 Best Practices for Building Effective Dashboards
        Practical, field-tested guidance for designing dashboards that communicate clearly.
      
    

    
      📝
      
        Tips for Creating Effective, Engaging Data Visualizations
        Core principles for turning data into clear and engaging visual stories.
      
    

  




  
    
    Blogs that Inspired
  
  

    
      💡
      
        The Information Lab
        Hands-on tutorials and ideas from one of the leading data consultancies.
      
    

    
      🎨
      
        DataRemixed
        Ben Jones on data visualisation, storytelling, and data literacy.
      
    

    
      📐
      
        PlayfairData
        Ryan Sleeper's detailed Tableau techniques, tips, and design walkthroughs.
      
    

    
      📋
      
        Data Revelations — Visualizing Survey Data
        
          Steve Wexler's guidance on visualising survey data. See also his
          resources.
        
      
    

  




  
    
    From Data to Tada
  
  

    
      🎥
      
        Using Data Visualization to Make Better Decisions—Faster
        Webinar on how visual analytics accelerates and improves decision-making.
      
    

    
      🧠
      
        17 Key Traits of Data Literacy
        Webinar breaking down the traits that define a data-literate organisation.
      
    

    
      🔄
      
        Inspiring Change with Data Visualization
        Webinar on using visualisation to drive understanding and action.
      
    

    
      🧩
      
        5 Important Elements of Dashboard Design
        Webinar covering the essential building blocks of a well-designed dashboard.
      
    

  




  
    
    Tableau Tips and Tricks
  
  

    
      ▶️
      
        Relationships, Joins and Unions in Tableau 2020.2
        YouTube video covering the data-management features introduced in Tableau 2020.2.

title: Hands-on Exercise 7b: Visualising and Analysing Text Data
text:
Learning Objectives:

understand tidytext framework for processing, analysing and visualising text data,
write function for importing multiple files into R,
combine multiple files into a single data frame,
clean and wrangle text data by using tidyverse approach,
visualise words with Word Cloud,
compute term frequency–inverse document frequency (TF-IDF) using tidytext method, and
visualising texts and terms relationship.






In this hands-on exercise, the following R packages for handling, processing, wrangling, analysing and visualising text data will be used:

tidytext, tidyverse (mainly readr, purrr, stringr, ggplot2)
widyr,
wordcloud and ggwordcloud,
textplot (required igraph, tidygraph and ggraph, )
DT,
lubridate and hms.

The code chunk:

pacman::p_load(tidytext, widyr, wordcloud, DT, ggwordcloud, textplot, lubridate, hms,
               tidyverse, tidygraph, ggraph, igraph)








news20 <- "data/20news/"





read_folder <- function(infolder) {
  tibble(file = dir(infolder, 
                    full.names = TRUE)) %>%
    mutate(text = map(file, 
                      read_lines)) %>%
    transmute(id = basename(file), 
              text) %>%
    unnest(text)
}








raw_text <- tibble(folder = 
                     dir(news20, 
                         full.names = TRUE)) %>%
  mutate(folder_out = map(folder, 
                          read_folder)) %>%
  unnest(cols = c(folder_out)) %>%
  transmute(newsgroup = basename(folder), 
            id, text)
write_rds(raw_text, "data/rds/news20.rds")


Things to learn from the code chunk above:

read_lines() of readr package is used to read up to n_max lines from a file.
map() of purrr package is used to transform their input by applying a function to each element of a list and returning an object of the same length as the input.
unnest() of dplyr package is used to flatten a list-column of data frames back out into regular columns.
mutate() of dplyr is used to add new variables and preserves existing ones;
transmute() of dplyr is used to add new variables and drops existing ones.
read_rds() is used to save the extracted and combined data frame as rds file for future use.





Code chunk below presents the frequency of messages by newsgroup.

raw_text %>%
  group_by(newsgroup) %>%
  summarize(messages = n_distinct(id)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(messages, newsgroup)) +
  geom_col(fill = "lightblue") +
  labs(y = NULL)












Using tidy data principles in processing, analysing and visualising text data.
Much of the infrastructure needed for text mining with tidy data frames already exists in packages like ‘dplyr’, ‘broom’, ‘tidyr’, and ‘ggplot2’.

Figure below shows the workflow using tidytext approach for processing and visualising text data.



Notice that each message has some structure and extra text that we don’t want to include in the analysis. For example, every message has a header, containing field such as “from:” or “in_reply_to:” that describe the message. Some also have automated email signatures, which occur after a line like “–”.

cleaned_text <- raw_text %>%
  group_by(newsgroup, id) %>%
  filter(cumsum(text == "") > 0,
         cumsum(str_detect(
           text, "^--")) == 0) %>%
  ungroup()

Things to learn from the code chunk:

cumsum() of base R is used to return a vector whose elements are the cumulative sums of the elements of the argument.
str_detect() from stringr is used to detect the presence or absence of a pattern in a string.




In this code chunk below, regular expressions are used to remove with nested text representing quotes from other users.

cleaned_text <- cleaned_text %>%
  filter(str_detect(text, "^[^>]+[A-Za-z\\d]")
         | text == "",
         !str_detect(text, 
                     "writes(:|\\.\\.\\.)$"),
         !str_detect(text, 
                     "^In article <")
  )

Things to learn from the code chunk:

str_detect() from stringr is used to detect the presence or absence of a pattern in a string.
filter() of dplyr package is used to subset a data frame, retaining all rows that satisfy the specified conditions.




In this code chunk below, unnest_tokens() of tidytext package is used to split the dataset into tokens, while stop_words() is used to remove stop-words.

usenet_words <- cleaned_text %>%
  unnest_tokens(word, text) %>%
  filter(str_detect(word, "[a-z']$"),
         !word %in% stop_words$word)

Now that we’ve removed the headers, signatures, and formatting, we can start exploring common words. For starters, we could find the most common words in the entire dataset, or within particular newsgroups.

usenet_words %>%
  count(word, sort = TRUE)

# A tibble: 5,542 × 2
   word           n
   <chr>      <int>
 1 people        57
 2 time          50
 3 jesus         47
 4 god           44
 5 message       40
 6 br            27
 7 bible         23
 8 drive         23
 9 homosexual    23
10 read          22
# ℹ 5,532 more rows


Instead of counting individual word, we can also count words within by newsgroup by using the code chunk below.

words_by_newsgroup <- usenet_words %>%
  count(newsgroup, word, sort = TRUE) %>%
  ungroup()
words_by_newsgroup

# A tibble: 8,124 × 3
   newsgroup          word            n
   <chr>              <chr>       <int>
 1 alt.atheism        jesus          39
 2 sci.crypt          message        31
 3 alt.atheism        br             27
 4 alt.atheism        rh             21
 5 sci.crypt          pad            20
 6 talk.politics.misc homosexual     20
 7 talk.politics.misc homosexuals    19
 8 comp.windows.x     widget         18
 9 misc.forsale       comics         17
10 rec.sport.hockey   pts            17
# ℹ 8,114 more rows





In this code chunk below, wordcloud() of wordcloud package is used to plot a static wordcloud.

wordcloud(words_by_newsgroup$word,
          words_by_newsgroup$n,
          max.words = 300)








A DT table can be used to complement the visual discovery.

DT::datatable(
  words_by_newsgroup,
  filter = "top",
  options = list(
    pageLength = 10,
    autoWidth = TRUE
  )
)








The wordcloud below is plotted by using ggwordcloud package.

set.seed(1234)

words_by_newsgroup %>%
  filter(n > 0) %>%
  ggplot(aes(label = word,
             size = n)) +
  geom_text_wordcloud() +
  theme_minimal() +
  facet_wrap(~newsgroup)













tf–idf, short for term frequency–inverse document frequency, is a numerical statistic that is intended to reflect how important a word is to a document in a collection of corpus.




The code chunk below uses bind_tf_idf() of tidytext to compute and bind the term frequency, inverse document frequency and ti-idf of a tidy text dataset to the dataset.

tf_idf <- words_by_newsgroup %>%
  bind_tf_idf(word, newsgroup, n) %>%
  arrange(desc(tf_idf))




Table below is an interactive table created by using datatable().

DT::datatable(
  tf_idf,
  filter = "top",                           
  options = list(
    pageLength = 10,                       
    autoWidth = TRUE
  )
) %>%
  DT::formatRound(columns = c("tf", "idf", "tf_idf"), digits = 3)








The code chunk below uses datatable() of DT package to create a html table that allows pagination of rows and columns.

DT::datatable(tf_idf, filter = 'top') %>% 
  formatRound(columns = c('tf', 'idf', 
                          'tf_idf'), 
              digits = 3) %>%
  formatStyle(0, 
              target = 'row', 
              lineHeight='25%')





Things to learn from the code chunk:

filter() argument is used to turn control the filter UI.
formatRound() is used to customise the values format. The argument digits define the number of decimal places.
formatStyle() is used to customise the output table. In this example, the arguments target and lineHeight are used to reduce the line height by 25%.

To learn more about customising DT’s table, visit this link.



Facet bar charts technique is used to visualise the tf-idf values of science related newsgroup.
The code chunk below plots the facet bar charts.

tf_idf %>%
  filter(str_detect(newsgroup, "^sci\\.")) %>%
  group_by(newsgroup) %>%
  slice_max(tf_idf, 
            n = 12) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(word = reorder(word, 
                        tf_idf)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(tf_idf, 
             word, 
             fill = newsgroup)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ newsgroup, 
             scales = "free") +
  labs(x = "tf-idf", 
       y = NULL)












To count the number of times that two words appear within the same document, or to see how correlated they are.
Most operations for finding pairwise counts or correlations need to turn the data into a wide matrix first.
widyr package first ‘casts’ a tidy dataset into a wide matrix, performs an operation such as a correlation on it, then re-tidies the result.


In this code chunk below, pairwise_cor() of widyr package is used to compute the correlation between newsgroup based on the common words found.


Show R code
newsgroup_cors <- words_by_newsgroup %>%
  pairwise_cor(newsgroup, 
               word, 
               n, 
               sort = TRUE)
newsgroup_cors


# A tibble: 380 × 3
   item1                    item2                    correlation
   <chr>                    <chr>                          <dbl>
 1 talk.religion.misc       soc.religion.christian         0.258
 2 soc.religion.christian   talk.religion.misc             0.258
 3 soc.religion.christian   alt.atheism                    0.207
 4 alt.atheism              soc.religion.christian         0.207
 5 comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware comp.sys.mac.hardware          0.204
 6 comp.sys.mac.hardware    comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware       0.204
 7 talk.religion.misc       alt.atheism                    0.170
 8 alt.atheism              talk.religion.misc             0.170
 9 comp.graphics            comp.os.ms-windows.misc        0.149
10 comp.os.ms-windows.misc  comp.graphics                  0.149
# ℹ 370 more rows





Now, we can visualise the relationship between newgroups in network graph as shown below.


Show R code
set.seed(2017)

newsgroup_cors %>%
  filter(correlation > .025) %>%
  graph_from_data_frame() %>%
  ggraph(layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(aes(alpha = correlation, 
                     width = correlation)) +
  geom_node_point(size = 6, 
                  color = "lightblue") +
  geom_node_text(aes(label = name),
                 color = "red",
                 repel = TRUE) +
  theme_void()












In this code chunk below, a bigram data frame is created by using unnest_tokens() of tidytext.

bigrams <- cleaned_text %>%
  unnest_tokens(bigram, 
                text, 
                token = "ngrams", 
                n = 2)
bigrams

# A tibble: 28,827 × 3
   newsgroup   id    bigram    
   <chr>       <chr> <chr>     
 1 alt.atheism 54256 <NA>      
 2 alt.atheism 54256 <NA>      
 3 alt.atheism 54256 as i      
 4 alt.atheism 54256 i don't   
 5 alt.atheism 54256 don't know
 6 alt.atheism 54256 know this 
 7 alt.atheism 54256 this book 
 8 alt.atheism 54256 book i    
 9 alt.atheism 54256 i will    
10 alt.atheism 54256 will use  
# ℹ 28,817 more rows





The code chunk is used to count and sort the bigram data frame ascendingly.

bigrams_count <- bigrams %>%
  filter(bigram != 'NA') %>%
  count(bigram, sort = TRUE)
bigrams_count

# A tibble: 19,888 × 2
   bigram       n
   <chr>    <int>
 1 of the     169
 2 in the     113
 3 to the      74
 4 to be       59
 5 for the     52
 6 i have      48
 7 that the    47
 8 if you      40
 9 on the      39
10 it is       38
# ℹ 19,878 more rows





The code chunk below is used to seperate the bigram into two words.

bigrams_separated <- bigrams %>%
  filter(bigram != 'NA') %>%
  separate(bigram, c("word1", "word2"), 
           sep = " ")

bigrams_filtered <- bigrams_separated %>%
  filter(!word1 %in% stop_words$word) %>%
  filter(!word2 %in% stop_words$word)
bigrams_separated

# A tibble: 27,530 × 4
   newsgroup   id    word1 word2  
   <chr>       <chr> <chr> <chr>  
 1 alt.atheism 54256 as    i      
 2 alt.atheism 54256 i     don't  
 3 alt.atheism 54256 don't know   
 4 alt.atheism 54256 know  this   
 5 alt.atheism 54256 this  book   
 6 alt.atheism 54256 book  i      
 7 alt.atheism 54256 i     will   
 8 alt.atheism 54256 will  use    
 9 alt.atheism 54256 use   your   
10 alt.atheism 54256 your  heresay
# ℹ 27,520 more rows






bigram_counts <- bigrams_filtered %>% 
  count(word1, word2, sort = TRUE)
bigram_counts

# A tibble: 4,138 × 3
   word1   word2     n
   <chr>   <chr> <int>
 1 1       2        12
 2 1       3        12
 3 static  void     10
 4 time    pad      10
 5 1       4         8
 6 infield fly       7
 7 mat     28        6
 8 vv      vv        6
 9 1       5         5
10 cock    crow      5
# ℹ 4,128 more rows





In the code chunk below, a network graph is created by using graph_from_data_frame() of igraph package.

bigram_graph <- bigram_counts %>%
  filter(n > 3) %>%
  graph_from_data_frame()
bigram_graph

IGRAPH 996709f DN-- 40 24 -- 
+ attr: name (v/c), n (e/n)
+ edges from 996709f (vertex names):
 [1] 1          ->2           1          ->3           static     ->void       
 [4] time       ->pad         1          ->4           infield    ->fly        
 [7] mat        ->28          vv         ->vv          1          ->5          
[10] cock       ->crow        noticeshell->widget      27         ->1993       
[13] 3          ->4           child      ->molestation cock       ->crew       
[16] gun        ->violence    heat       ->sink        homosexual ->male       
[19] homosexual ->women       include    ->xol         mary       ->magdalene  
[22] read       ->write       rev        ->20          tt         ->ee         





In this code chunk below, ggraph package is used to plot the bigram.


Show R code
set.seed(1234)

ggraph(bigram_graph, layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link() +
  geom_node_point() +
  geom_node_text(aes(label = name), 
                 vjust = 1, 
                 hjust = 1)














Show R code
set.seed(1234)

a <- grid::arrow(type = "closed", 
                 length = unit(.15,
                               "inches"))

ggraph(bigram_graph, 
       layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(aes(edge_alpha = n), 
                 show.legend = FALSE,
                 arrow = a, 
                 end_cap = circle(.07,
                                  'inches')) +
  geom_node_point(color = "lightblue", 
                  size = 5) +
  geom_node_text(aes(label = name), 
                 vjust = 1, 
                 hjust = 1) +
  theme_void()
















Reference guide

widyr: Widen, process, and re-tidy a dataset
United Nations Voting Correlations

Learning Objectives:

understand tidytext framework for processing, analysing and visualising text data,

clean and wrangle text data by using tidyverse approach,
visualise words with Word Cloud,
compute term frequency–inverse document frequency (TF-IDF) using tidytext method, and
visualising texts and terms relationship.

title: Hands-on Exercise 7b: Visualising and Analysing Text Data
text:
In this hands-on exercise, the following R packages for handling, processing, wrangling, analysing and visualising text data will be used:

tidytext, tidyverse (mainly readr, purrr, stringr, ggplot2)
widyr,
wordcloud and ggwordcloud,
textplot (required igraph, tidygraph and ggraph, )
DT,
lubridate and hms.

The code chunk:

pacman::p_load(tidytext, widyr, wordcloud, DT, ggwordcloud, textplot, lubridate, hms,
               tidyverse, tidygraph, ggraph, igraph)

title: Hands-on Exercise 7b: Visualising and Analysing Text Data
text:
news20 <- "data/20news/"





read_folder <- function(infolder) {
  tibble(file = dir(infolder, 
                    full.names = TRUE)) %>%
    mutate(text = map(file, 
                      read_lines)) %>%
    transmute(id = basename(file), 
              text) %>%
    unnest(text)
}
crumbs: 15 · Visualising and Analysing Text Data with R: tidytext methods

title: Hands-on Exercise 7b: Visualising and Analysing Text Data
text:
raw_text <- tibble(folder = 
                     dir(news20, 
                         full.names = TRUE)) %>%
  mutate(folder_out = map(folder, 
                          read_folder)) %>%
  unnest(cols = c(folder_out)) %>%
  transmute(newsgroup = basename(folder), 
            id, text)
write_rds(raw_text, "data/rds/news20.rds")


Things to learn from the code chunk above:

read_lines() of readr package is used to read up to n_max lines from a file.
map() of purrr package is used to transform their input by applying a function to each element of a list and returning an object of the same length as the input.
unnest() of dplyr package is used to flatten a list-column of data frames back out into regular columns.
mutate() of dplyr is used to add new variables and preserves existing ones;
transmute() of dplyr is used to add new variables and drops existing ones.
read_rds() is used to save the extracted and combined data frame as rds file for future use.
crumbs: 15 · Visualising and Analysing Text Data with R: tidytext methods

title: Hands-on Exercise 7b: Visualising and Analysing Text Data
text:
Code chunk below presents the frequency of messages by newsgroup.

raw_text %>%
  group_by(newsgroup) %>%
  summarize(messages = n_distinct(id)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(messages, newsgroup)) +
  geom_col(fill = "lightblue") +
  labs(y = NULL)
crumbs: 15 · Visualising and Analysing Text Data with R: tidytext methods

title: Hands-on Exercise 7b: Visualising and Analysing Text Data
text:
Using tidy data principles in processing, analysing and visualising text data.
Much of the infrastructure needed for text mining with tidy data frames already exists in packages like ‘dplyr’, ‘broom’, ‘tidyr’, and ‘ggplot2’.

Figure below shows the workflow using tidytext approach for processing and visualising text data.



Notice that each message has some structure and extra text that we don’t want to include in the analysis. For example, every message has a header, containing field such as “from:” or “in_reply_to:” that describe the message. Some also have automated email signatures, which occur after a line like “–”.

cleaned_text <- raw_text %>%
  group_by(newsgroup, id) %>%
  filter(cumsum(text == "") > 0,
         cumsum(str_detect(
           text, "^--")) == 0) %>%
  ungroup()

Things to learn from the code chunk:

cumsum() of base R is used to return a vector whose elements are the cumulative sums of the elements of the argument.
str_detect() from stringr is used to detect the presence or absence of a pattern in a string.




In this code chunk below, regular expressions are used to remove with nested text representing quotes from other users.

cleaned_text <- cleaned_text %>%
  filter(str_detect(text, "^[^>]+[A-Za-z\\d]")
         | text == "",
         !str_detect(text, 
                     "writes(:|\\.\\.\\.)$"),
         !str_detect(text, 
                     "^In article <")
  )

Things to learn from the code chunk:

str_detect() from stringr is used to detect the presence or absence of a pattern in a string.
filter() of dplyr package is used to subset a data frame, retaining all rows that satisfy the specified conditions.




In this code chunk below, unnest_tokens() of tidytext package is used to split the dataset into tokens, while stop_words() is used to remove stop-words.

usenet_words <- cleaned_text %>%
  unnest_tokens(word, text) %>%
  filter(str_detect(word, "[a-z']$"),
         !word %in% stop_words$word)

Now that we’ve removed the headers, signatures, and formatting, we can start exploring common words. For starters, we could find the most common words in the entire dataset, or within particular newsgroups.

usenet_words %>%
  count(word, sort = TRUE)

# A tibble: 5,542 × 2
   word           n
   <chr>      <int>
 1 people        57
 2 time          50
 3 jesus         47
 4 god           44
 5 message       40
 6 br            27
 7 bible         23
 8 drive         23
 9 homosexual    23
10 read          22
# ℹ 5,532 more rows


Instead of counting individual word, we can also count words within by newsgroup by using the code chunk below.

words_by_newsgroup <- usenet_words %>%
  count(newsgroup, word, sort = TRUE) %>%
  ungroup()
words_by_newsgroup

# A tibble: 8,124 × 3
   newsgroup          word            n
   <chr>              <chr>       <int>
 1 alt.atheism        jesus          39
 2 sci.crypt          message        31
 3 alt.atheism        br             27
 4 alt.atheism        rh             21
 5 sci.crypt          pad            20
 6 talk.politics.misc homosexual     20
 7 talk.politics.misc homosexuals    19
 8 comp.windows.x     widget         18
 9 misc.forsale       comics         17
10 rec.sport.hockey   pts            17
# ℹ 8,114 more rows





In this code chunk below, wordcloud() of wordcloud package is used to plot a static wordcloud.

wordcloud(words_by_newsgroup$word,
          words_by_newsgroup$n,
          max.words = 300)








A DT table can be used to complement the visual discovery.

DT::datatable(
  words_by_newsgroup,
  filter = "top",
  options = list(
    pageLength = 10,
    autoWidth = TRUE
  )
)








The wordcloud below is plotted by using ggwordcloud package.

set.seed(1234)

words_by_newsgroup %>%
  filter(n > 0) %>%
  ggplot(aes(label = word,
             size = n)) +
  geom_text_wordcloud() +
  theme_minimal() +
  facet_wrap(~newsgroup)
crumbs: 15 · Visualising and Analysing Text Data with R: tidytext methods

title: Hands-on Exercise 7b: Visualising and Analysing Text Data
text:
tf–idf, short for term frequency–inverse document frequency, is a numerical statistic that is intended to reflect how important a word is to a document in a collection of corpus.




The code chunk below uses bind_tf_idf() of tidytext to compute and bind the term frequency, inverse document frequency and ti-idf of a tidy text dataset to the dataset.

tf_idf <- words_by_newsgroup %>%
  bind_tf_idf(word, newsgroup, n) %>%
  arrange(desc(tf_idf))




Table below is an interactive table created by using datatable().

DT::datatable(
  tf_idf,
  filter = "top",                           
  options = list(
    pageLength = 10,                       
    autoWidth = TRUE
  )
) %>%
  DT::formatRound(columns = c("tf", "idf", "tf_idf"), digits = 3)








The code chunk below uses datatable() of DT package to create a html table that allows pagination of rows and columns.

DT::datatable(tf_idf, filter = 'top') %>% 
  formatRound(columns = c('tf', 'idf', 
                          'tf_idf'), 
              digits = 3) %>%
  formatStyle(0, 
              target = 'row', 
              lineHeight='25%')





Things to learn from the code chunk:

filter() argument is used to turn control the filter UI.
formatRound() is used to customise the values format. The argument digits define the number of decimal places.
formatStyle() is used to customise the output table. In this example, the arguments target and lineHeight are used to reduce the line height by 25%.

To learn more about customising DT’s table, visit this link.



Facet bar charts technique is used to visualise the tf-idf values of science related newsgroup.
The code chunk below plots the facet bar charts.

tf_idf %>%
  filter(str_detect(newsgroup, "^sci\\.")) %>%
  group_by(newsgroup) %>%
  slice_max(tf_idf, 
            n = 12) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(word = reorder(word, 
                        tf_idf)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(tf_idf, 
             word, 
             fill = newsgroup)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ newsgroup, 
             scales = "free") +
  labs(x = "tf-idf", 
       y = NULL)












To count the number of times that two words appear within the same document, or to see how correlated they are.
Most operations for finding pairwise counts or correlations need to turn the data into a wide matrix first.
widyr package first ‘casts’ a tidy dataset into a wide matrix, performs an operation such as a correlation on it, then re-tidies the result.


In this code chunk below, pairwise_cor() of widyr package is used to compute the correlation between newsgroup based on the common words found.


Show R code
newsgroup_cors <- words_by_newsgroup %>%
  pairwise_cor(newsgroup, 
               word, 
               n, 
               sort = TRUE)
newsgroup_cors


# A tibble: 380 × 3
   item1                    item2                    correlation
   <chr>                    <chr>                          <dbl>
 1 talk.religion.misc       soc.religion.christian         0.258
 2 soc.religion.christian   talk.religion.misc             0.258
 3 soc.religion.christian   alt.atheism                    0.207
 4 alt.atheism              soc.religion.christian         0.207
 5 comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware comp.sys.mac.hardware          0.204
 6 comp.sys.mac.hardware    comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware       0.204
 7 talk.religion.misc       alt.atheism                    0.170
 8 alt.atheism              talk.religion.misc             0.170
 9 comp.graphics            comp.os.ms-windows.misc        0.149
10 comp.os.ms-windows.misc  comp.graphics                  0.149
# ℹ 370 more rows





Now, we can visualise the relationship between newgroups in network graph as shown below.


Show R code
set.seed(2017)

newsgroup_cors %>%
  filter(correlation > .025) %>%
  graph_from_data_frame() %>%
  ggraph(layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(aes(alpha = correlation, 
                     width = correlation)) +
  geom_node_point(size = 6, 
                  color = "lightblue") +
  geom_node_text(aes(label = name),
                 color = "red",
                 repel = TRUE) +
  theme_void()












In this code chunk below, a bigram data frame is created by using unnest_tokens() of tidytext.

bigrams <- cleaned_text %>%
  unnest_tokens(bigram, 
                text, 
                token = "ngrams", 
                n = 2)
bigrams

# A tibble: 28,827 × 3
   newsgroup   id    bigram    
   <chr>       <chr> <chr>     
 1 alt.atheism 54256 <NA>      
 2 alt.atheism 54256 <NA>      
 3 alt.atheism 54256 as i      
 4 alt.atheism 54256 i don't   
 5 alt.atheism 54256 don't know
 6 alt.atheism 54256 know this 
 7 alt.atheism 54256 this book 
 8 alt.atheism 54256 book i    
 9 alt.atheism 54256 i will    
10 alt.atheism 54256 will use  
# ℹ 28,817 more rows





The code chunk is used to count and sort the bigram data frame ascendingly.

bigrams_count <- bigrams %>%
  filter(bigram != 'NA') %>%
  count(bigram, sort = TRUE)
bigrams_count

# A tibble: 19,888 × 2
   bigram       n
   <chr>    <int>
 1 of the     169
 2 in the     113
 3 to the      74
 4 to be       59
 5 for the     52
 6 i have      48
 7 that the    47
 8 if you      40
 9 on the      39
10 it is       38
# ℹ 19,878 more rows





The code chunk below is used to seperate the bigram into two words.

bigrams_separated <- bigrams %>%
  filter(bigram != 'NA') %>%
  separate(bigram, c("word1", "word2"), 
           sep = " ")

bigrams_filtered <- bigrams_separated %>%
  filter(!word1 %in% stop_words$word) %>%
  filter(!word2 %in% stop_words$word)
bigrams_separated

# A tibble: 27,530 × 4
   newsgroup   id    word1 word2  
   <chr>       <chr> <chr> <chr>  
 1 alt.atheism 54256 as    i      
 2 alt.atheism 54256 i     don't  
 3 alt.atheism 54256 don't know   
 4 alt.atheism 54256 know  this   
 5 alt.atheism 54256 this  book   
 6 alt.atheism 54256 book  i      
 7 alt.atheism 54256 i     will   
 8 alt.atheism 54256 will  use    
 9 alt.atheism 54256 use   your   
10 alt.atheism 54256 your  heresay
# ℹ 27,520 more rows






bigram_counts <- bigrams_filtered %>% 
  count(word1, word2, sort = TRUE)
bigram_counts

# A tibble: 4,138 × 3
   word1   word2     n
   <chr>   <chr> <int>
 1 1       2        12
 2 1       3        12
 3 static  void     10
 4 time    pad      10
 5 1       4         8
 6 infield fly       7
 7 mat     28        6
 8 vv      vv        6
 9 1       5         5
10 cock    crow      5
# ℹ 4,128 more rows





In the code chunk below, a network graph is created by using graph_from_data_frame() of igraph package.

bigram_graph <- bigram_counts %>%
  filter(n > 3) %>%
  graph_from_data_frame()
bigram_graph

IGRAPH 996709f DN-- 40 24 -- 
+ attr: name (v/c), n (e/n)
+ edges from 996709f (vertex names):
 [1] 1          ->2           1          ->3           static     ->void       
 [4] time       ->pad         1          ->4           infield    ->fly        
 [7] mat        ->28          vv         ->vv          1          ->5          
[10] cock       ->crow        noticeshell->widget      27         ->1993       
[13] 3          ->4           child      ->molestation cock       ->crew       
[16] gun        ->violence    heat       ->sink        homosexual ->male       
[19] homosexual ->women       include    ->xol         mary       ->magdalene  
[22] read       ->write       rev        ->20          tt         ->ee         





In this code chunk below, ggraph package is used to plot the bigram.


Show R code
set.seed(1234)

ggraph(bigram_graph, layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link() +
  geom_node_point() +
  geom_node_text(aes(label = name), 
                 vjust = 1, 
                 hjust = 1)














Show R code
set.seed(1234)

a <- grid::arrow(type = "closed", 
                 length = unit(.15,
                               "inches"))

ggraph(bigram_graph, 
       layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(aes(edge_alpha = n), 
                 show.legend = FALSE,
                 arrow = a, 
                 end_cap = circle(.07,
                                  'inches')) +
  geom_node_point(color = "lightblue", 
                  size = 5) +
  geom_node_text(aes(label = name), 
                 vjust = 1, 
                 hjust = 1) +
  theme_void()

title: R Resources
text:
A curated set of references used throughout ISSS608 Visual Analytics and Applications.
  These resources cover the R programming ecosystem, the tidyverse, and modern data visualisation practices.




  
    
    Getting Started with R
  
  

    
      📘
      
        R for Data Science
        
          Garrett Grolemund & Hadley Wickham. The definitive guide to the tidyverse —
          covers data import, wrangling, visualisation, and modelling workflows.
        
      
    

    
      📗
      
        Modern R with the tidyverse
        
          Bruno Rodrigues. Practical introduction to modern R programming.
          Chapter 2
          offers a thorough overview of R data objects and types.
        
      
    

    
      📙
      
        Introduction to R – tidyverse
        
          Brendan R. E. Ansell. A beginner-friendly course book focused on tidy data principles
          and practical data manipulation with dplyr and tidyr.
        
      
    

    
      📦
      
        Installing R Packages from CRAN, Bioconductor, GitHub and Co.
        
          Comprehensive guide to installing R packages from multiple sources,
          including common troubleshooting tips.
        
      
    

  




  
    
    Workflow & Best Practices
  
  

    
      ⚙️
      
        R Workflow
        
          Frank Harrell's guide to reproducible, project-oriented R workflows —
          covers project structure, naming conventions, and literate programming.
        
      
    

    
      ✍️
      
        R Workflow — Blog Post
        
          Statistical Thinking blog by Frank Harrell. A readable summary of
          the R Workflow principles with practical advice for analysts.
